misconceptions.claim: Reed-Solomon just repeats the message with extra copies.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Error-correcting codes are often introduced as redundancy, so the polynomial structure can disappear.

misconceptions.claim: A longer codeword makes cheating harder only because there are more places to check.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Sampling more positions is intuitive, but the real guarantee comes from the low-degree agreement bound.

misconceptions.claim: A few agreeing positions mean two encoded messages are probably the same.
misconceptions.why_tempting: Local agreement feels persuasive before the distance bound is internalized.

misconceptions.claim: Reed-Solomon belongs only to storage, not proof systems.
misconceptions.why_tempting: The coding-theory origin hides its later role as the proximity object inside FRI.

dumb_questions: Where did the extra symbols come from?
Why evaluate the polynomial instead of sending coefficients directly?
How many positions can two different messages agree on?
What does distance have to do with a random spot-check?
bad_intuitions_to_kill: Redundancy is copying; replace with evaluation of one low-degree polynomial at many points.
Distance is a vague robustness word; replace with a count of positions where two codewords must differ.
Reed-Solomon is separate from ZK; replace with FRI and low-degree proximity as the downstream role.
